chapters.title: Thriving matters more than surviving
chapters.text:
Living in the 21st century is all fun and games until it's really not. Our time is the time of stress and pressure. The age of technologies and globalized society has brought challenges that were unknown to our ancestors. Managing your life and remaining happy and sane at the same time is a challenge that all of us must face nowadays. So how do we do that? Thankfully, Carey Nieuwhof has the answers.

Stress evolves with time and adapts to social climate like a virus. Modern stress looks different from what earlier centuries produced.

Most problems share a root: too much work, too many distractions, too many options. Abundance makes life hard to manage, and you may feel there is no room to breathe. Living under pressure long enough causes damage that eventually appears as burnout — general unhappiness, low motivation, and constant exhaustion.

More than seventy percent of 7,500 surveyed full-time American employees have experienced burnout on some level. The condition is so common that society treats it as inevitable. Yet you do not have to burn out. You can have the professional and domestic life you want and still feel rested, energized, and happy — and this is not a superhuman-only recipe.

The following chapters explain stress, how to avoid it, and a simple method: do your best work when you are at your best, to turn life around.

Doing what you're best at when you're at your best unlocks potential and freedom on a scale that shocks a lot of people who try it. It has the potential to change everything. ~ Carey Nieuwhof

chapters.title: Stress as the decade's default setting
chapters.text:
There's a false idea that burnout occurs when you are not doing what makes you happy. The truth is that even the ideal life you have carefully constructed can turn on you. You might have the perfect job and the dream family but still feel stressed and tired.

Usually burnout results from inability to prioritize correctly and distribute time and energy, your most valuable resources. An unsustainable pace may advance a career but will not make you happy; sooner or later damage appears.

Constant stress takes a real toll: chronic pain, panic attacks, headaches, breathing difficulties, memory problems, slower reaction time, reduced sexual desire, and other disorders. This is only a short list of what stress can do when it controls life.

Stress is labeled negative yet often glorified and rewarded. If you are not stressed, you may feel you are not working enough to succeed. Stress has woven itself into daily life as the default. Yet merely surviving each day is neither cool nor fun. You owe it to yourself to reduce what brings you down.

You need not uproot life and move to the forest. Keep your current life and build on it by restructuring routine and creating new habits. Traditional time management is not enough. Top performers manage three assets: time, energy, and priorities — doing what you are best at when energy is highest.

Doing what you're best at when you are at your best is the method that will unlock your potential and give you the freedom to change your life.

You may feel everything works against you — never enough time or energy — yet you control those resources more than they control you.

chapters.title: Breaking cycles that feed burnout
chapters.text:
The unsustainable pace of life is usually a result of negative patterns we fall into. Considering that all of us are creatures of habit, they can be challenging to break without a solid plan. The most damaging patterns can suck us into the stress spiral — a permanent state of overwhelming exhaustion. The stress spiral is a combination of three major patterns:

• Unfocused time.
• Unleveraged energy.
• Hijacked priorities.

Together they can throw life off the rails. Examine each.

Unfocused time means the daily routine has no structure, resulting in always feeling like there is not enough time. Our failure to focus time comes from treating all hours of the day the same way and completing tasks randomly without paying attention to the clock. As a result, we may waste our most productive hours on trivial things that do not require much focus or thought.

Energy is another resource we often fail to utilize efficiently. It is no secret that energy levels fluctuate throughout the day. Unleveraged energy results from our inability to cooperate with these levels and divide tasks accordingly.

Last but certainly not least are hijacked priorities. The problem stems from our inability to say no. Allowing other people to decide what and when we get done means letting them put their priorities above ours. This is a common issue and one of the main factors that can pull people into the stress spiral.

Though difficult, these patterns are not permanent. Substitute the stress spiral with the thrive cycle through three new habits:

• Focus time.
• Leverage energy.
• Realize priorities.

If stress consists of behavioral patterns, so does thriving. Understanding them helps build better habits.

chapters.title: You have more time than you think
chapters.text:
Fast, eventful lives make us feel time is scarce. Something always gets postponed — work, rest, family, hobbies. Whatever suffers, negative impact follows. The thrive cycle means thriving in every aspect, not just one or two, so everything necessary must fit the timetable.

Everyone receives the same twenty-four hours per day. Your boss, parents, top earners, and you all share that allotment. Productivity differences come from how time is used.

Time doesn't discriminate. With the two exceptions of the day you're born and the day you die, everyone gets exactly the same amount of time every day. ~ Carey Nieuwhof

Fixing unfocused time requires more than traditional management. Start with a mental shift: embrace passion and abandon balance as the ultimate goal.

Balanced life sounds ideal yet is often an unreachable pipe dream. Current notions of balance mean less effort and fewer commitments — not a road to success.

Concentrate on passion instead. Hours are limited, so spend them on what matters with whole-hearted enthusiasm — which also limits how much you can take on.

People who achieve great things are passionate, not balanced.

Practical exercise: list up to five activities you want time for and estimate weekly hours beside each. Keep the list as you continue.

chapters.title: Cooperate with your body's rhythms
chapters.text:
Though you have the same quantity of hours as everyone else, those hours are not the same. Some are more appropriate for intellectual activities, others not so much. This has to do with energy levels that change throughout the day. You can't be productive and at your best for eight or ten hours per day. If you pay attention to your body and mood, you'll notice that there are no more than five hours throughout a day when you are truly thriving and ready to tackle any tasks thrown your way. You have to accept these limitations and treasure these three to five hours — they are your green zone and the key to being productive.

Understanding and knowing your green zone will help you plan your day accordingly. The key to figuring out your green zone is tracking your mood and productivity. First, analyze your typical day: when do you feel the most productive and in high spirits? Next, pay attention to your body; you'll notice the difference between your green zone and the rest of the day. It might also be helpful to get input from colleagues, friends, and family; they usually notice your highs and lows.

In the daily green zone, your energy is high, your mind is sharp and focused, and it's easy to work on creative tasks that require critical thinking and imagination.

Once you've figured out your green zone, think about the rest of the day. Two more zones are hiding somewhere in there — yellow and red. The yellow zone is when your energy level is somewhere in the middle, and it is at its lowest in the red zone. These zones can be utilized just as effectively as your green zone if you use them for energy-appropriate tasks. For example, your red zone is the perfect time for exercising or chores, while the yellow zone is suitable for meetings and social events. But it's all about learning what works for you. So, experiment with your zones and figure out what tasks are best for them.

This is a good time to return to your list from the previous chapter and divide the tasks you've written down between your zones.

Did you know? About twenty-one percent of people are night owls, fourteen percent are morning people, and sixty-five percent fall between.

chapters.title: Defend your true priorities
chapters.text:
Your time and energy are the most valuable resources at your disposal. And as with financial assets, making the right investments is extremely important. This means knowing when, how, and what to spend them on. We have already covered time and energy distribution. Now let's move on to the last ingredient — your priorities.

Getting your priorities to work in your favor isn't an easy task. It requires defining them to yourself and defending them when others try to hijack them.

The wrong things will always want your attention. It's your job to focus on the right things. ~ Carey Nieuwhof

Every person you meet tries to distract you from your priorities. Every call, text, and request pulls you off plan. The process works both ways — everyone needs others' time and energy for their goals. Prioritize your priorities, not everyone else's. Healthy boundaries help.

Divide tasks into categories:

• Urgent but not important (random call or knock on the door)
• Urgent and important (deadlines, emergencies)
• Important but not urgent (health, goals, resolutions)
• Neither important nor urgent (doomscrolling or web surfing)

Notice how much time goes to items that are neither urgent nor important when assigning tasks to energy zones.

Smartphones create constant distraction. For green-zone work, use do-not-disturb and an optimal environment.

Distracted you is your worst enemy. Therefore, we need an appropriate distraction-free environment to thrive.

Revolutionizing routine can be simple: do what you are best at and passionate about during your green zone in distraction-free conditions. When those align, you enter the thrive cycle.

Did you know? On average, people touch their phones 2,617 times throughout the day.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
If you follow the simple rules described in this summary, you'll immediately notice the changes in your life and your mindset. The thrive cycle allows you to lead a healthy and manageable lifestyle and creates the necessary room to breathe. Of course, the number of your responsibilities won't shrink, and you won't get more hours in your day, but because time, energy, and priorities will be working in your favor, it will certainly feel like that.

Sadly, none of this means that your life will be free of stress; it'll simply become more manageable and easier to overcome. You'll build healthier connections with your friends, family, and coworkers by creating a healthy routine. As you work with time, energy, and priorities to break the old patterns of the stress spiral, you will better the quality of your life and improve your relationships.

The thrive cycle is about being at your best — less about what you accomplish and more about creating space to become your best self. When life no longer feels like collapse, self-reflection and improvement come easier, ensuring success and peace of mind.

In the end, who you're becoming is so much more important than what you're doing. It's time you stepped into the future. You're going to love the person you meet. ~ Carey Nieuwhof

Try this

• Visit Carey Nieuwhof's website www.AtYourBestToday.com to find all the necessary charts and templates that will aid in your journey toward the thrive cycle.
• Map out your energy zones and the appropriate tasks in your calendar of choice.
• Fill out your calendar one or two months in advance to have a clearer picture of your plans.

Read it till the end?
